clean_story:
Stretched seconds. Shrunken hours.
Memory warps time like a rubber band—pulling moments apart or squeezing them together.

The brain doesn’t record time like a clock. It stitches moments using emotion, surprise, and repetition. A boring lecture drags because memory logs few details—like a camera taking one blurry photo per minute. But a near-miss car crash? Memory snaps hundreds of sharp images, stretching those seconds into what feels like minutes.

This distortion has a name: duration neglect. Tests show people remember painful experiences (like dental work) as shorter if the worst pain came early. The brain averages the peaks, ignoring how long they lasted. Even weirder: Repeated memories—like retelling a vacation story—get compressed. Each recall trims details, making a week-long trip feel like a weekend.

Three related subjects are false memories in court, how dreams bend time, and why nostalgia speeds up years.
